query: Four U.S.-based software companies (NorthWave Inc., Orion Systems LLC, ClearPeak Corp. and HarborSoft Ltd.) assert that R. Silva, who until April 2019 worked in the Milan development unit of Orion Systems and thereafter was hired by the Swiss firm Lumen (CH) Sàrl, copied the source code and confidential documentation for three programs (codenamed A1, B3 and B4) and that Lumen (CH) Sàrl intends to exploit those materials. The companies ask the cantonal authorities in Lausanne for interim relief consisting of an injunction prohibiting use, reproduction or disclosure of the programs and the immediate seizure and forensic inspection of computers and storage media located at Lumen (CH) Sàrl and in R. Silva’s possession, alleging violations of copyright, trade‑secret law and unfair competition; is a Swiss cantonal authority competent to grant such provisional measures, have the applicants made plausible their status as rights‑holders or other standing to bring these claims, and, applying the usual legal criteria for interim relief (a prima facie right, interference or risk thereof, urgency/harm that is difficult to repair and proportionality), should provisional measures be ordered?

On 9 August 2011 a 62‑year‑old cyclist (the claimant) was struck at a junction inside an industrial estate by a delivery van that had been leaving a side access to turn left onto a county road; the cyclist sustained a head injury, a cervical sprain and later had his loss of earning capacity assessed at 45% by the State Disability Office (decision of 10 November 2014). The cyclist brought an action against the van driver and the insurer on 15 October 2013 for damages and enlarged his claims on 12 March 2015 and 20 April 2016 to include past lost earnings and impairment of his economic future. The defendants maintain that these later claims are time‑barred under Art. 83 SVG because the claimant could have been aware of his damage and its extent earlier, and alternatively assert that the cyclist rode against the traffic flow and that this alleged conduct constitutes gross negligence exonerating the vehicle‑holder under Art. 59 Abs. 1 SVG. Having regard to the contemporaneous medical records, a neuropsychological expert assessment dated February 2012 (communicated in June 2012) and the State Disability Office decision of November 2014, are the additional claims made in 2015 and 2016 prescribed within the meaning of Art. 83 SVG; and, if the cyclist did in fact ride counter‑flow, does that behaviour amount to gross negligence sufficient to exonerate the vehicle‑holder under Art. 59 Abs. 1 SVG?

On 12 March 2012, Meridian Leasing Ltd and Orion Transport LLC entered into a three-year vehicle hire agreement for a box truck explicitly designated for business operations; Mr. Carter, the managing partner of Orion Transport, signed the contract personally as a joint and several obligor. After Orion Transport entered insolvency on 30 September 2012, Mr. Carter delivered the truck back to Meridian on 15 October 2012; Meridian inspected the vehicle on 22 November 2012 and invoiced refurbishment expenses of CHF 9,200 (CHF 9,000 of which were described as wear beyond the normal degree). Meridian terminated the lease on 10 December 2012 and sought CHF 18,500; by letter dated 5 January 2013 Meridian unilaterally offered to reduce its claim by CHF 4,500 and demanded payment of CHF 14,000 by 12 January 2013, an offer that Mr. Carter did not accept. Mr. Carter contests both the refurbishment charges (arguing Meridian failed to verify and notify the truck's condition upon return) and the contention that Meridian's 5 January 2013 communication effected a conventional remission of CHF 4,500. In this situation, (1) does Meridian's 5 January 2013 offer amount to a conventional remission of debt without Mr. Carter's express acceptance? (2) Because the hire agreement concerned a vehicle for business use, does the Consumer Credit Act (LCC) apply, and if the LCC does not apply, may Meridian recover the refurbishment costs despite not having carried out an immediate inspection and immediate notification of defects at return (in particular with regard to a subsidiary application of Art. 267a CO)?

A publicly listed manufacturing company, Orion Manufacturing AG, was served with a payment order on 14 January 2022 relating to several outstanding invoices, against which it entered a total opposition. The creditor, Delta Supplies Ltd., applied to have the opposition lifted on 30 January 2022 and obtained an order lifting the opposition; the operative part of that order was rendered on 5 September 2022 and the reasons were notified on 20 November 2022. Delta Supplies subsequently filed a petition to place Orion Manufacturing into bankruptcy on 1 February 2024. Orion Manufacturing contests the validity of that bankruptcy petition, contending that the peremptory fifteen‑month period under Art. 166 Abs. 2 SchKG had expired, and alternatively asserting solvency by producing an extract from the debt enforcement register. In that context: (1) Was the bankruptcy petition of 1 February 2024 lodged within the peremptory period of Art. 166 Abs. 2 SchKG when the suspension of that period during the lifting‑of‑opposition proceedings is taken into account, and precisely at what point does that suspension end — upon notification of the operative dispositive alone or only upon notification of the full reasoned decision? (2) In an appeal against the opening of bankruptcy, can the debtor obtain annulment of the bankruptcy solely on the basis of an extract from the debt enforcement register that makes solvency appear probable?

A logistics company (R Ltd.), which owns a distribution warehouse, its facility manager (S Ltd.), and the warehouse’s sole shareholder-director (T) signed on 14 March 2012 a written “mortgage lending arrangement” under which S Ltd. extended credit to R Ltd. (and to T and U Ltd.) and took as security a bearer mortgage certificate for CHF 420,000 encumbering plot no. 2207; the certificate, created on 20 March 2012, contains no designation of any obligor and was handed over in full ownership to S Ltd. Various management advances were posted to an account producing an alleged debit balance of CHF 401,237.88; S Ltd. issued a formal demand and on 9 September 2013 denounced the bearer certificate with effect from 1 February 2014; S Ltd. then applied to enforce the real‑estate charge by realization and obtained provisional release for up to CHF 420,000. T and R Ltd. filed oppositions, T disputing his status as a debtor and R Ltd. challenging the amount sought. Under these circumstances, can S Ltd., as holder of a bearer mortgage certificate that does not identify a debtor, validly pursue enforcement by realization of the real‑estate pledge for CHF 420,000 against T and/or R Ltd., or is recovery limited to the amount of the underlying claim; did the bearer certificate operate as a novation of the prior debt or was it transferred as a fiduciary security (thus excluding novation), and accordingly what defenses can the putative debtors raise (including as to interest), and were the denunciation and the alleged date of exigibility validly effected?

A vehicle owner born in 1963 had a replacement wheel assembly fitted on 12 September 2002 that incorporated a metal rotor and a polymer friction pad supplied by manufacturer C. Routine vehicle inspections in 2005 and again in October 2008 showed unusual, accelerated wear of the friction pad, and the owner paid for a corrective replacement of the assembly on 21 March 2009. The owner commenced proceedings against the successor company Z Ltd in 2011, claiming compensation for repair costs and non‑pecuniary harm, alleging the friction pad was defective (premature wear); the producer replies the action is time‑barred and, on the merits, that no defect within the meaning of the Federal Act on Product Liability (LRFP) has been established and that no promise as to service life was given, and in any event it is exonerated under the exculpatory proof provisions of the PrHG (in particular Art. 5 PrHG (in particular lit. b and e)). On the basis of the available evidence — inspection photographs, an expert report prepared without production of the removed pad for testing, and technical publications describing similar late failures — has the claimant carried the burden of proving a defect under Art. 4 PrHG, and if not, can the manufacturer validly free itself from liability under Art. 5 PrHG (in particular lit. b and e) for the losses alleged?

A long‑standing patient of Dr. Rivera, an ophthalmic surgeon who has been treating her since 2002, agreed in June 2016 to the insertion of a corneal inlay. Dr. Rivera performed a preparatory debridement without ordering corneal imaging (e.g., OCT) or conducting preoperative sensitivity tests, fitted a temporary inlay and then implanted a permanent device on 14 September 2016; the patient thereafter suffered severe ocular pain and conjunctival inflammation. Dr. Rivera removed the permanent inlay and carried out incomplete corneal procedures, while a different eye specialist later undertook full re‑treatment and other necessary care at costs paid by the patient. The patient now seeks reimbursement of the expenses paid to the second specialist and the return of a deposit, alleging the initial treatment breached the applicable professional standard of care; Dr. Rivera, by way of counterclaim, demands payment for the services he performed and for placement of a permanent inlay that was never completed because the patient lost confidence. In these circumstances: (1) Should the ophthalmic services provided be characterized as a mandate (contract of mandate) rather than as a work contract? (2) Did Dr. Rivera violate the duty of care arising from such a mandate by omitting the imaging and preoperative tests and by performing incomplete procedures, thereby incurring contractual liability and obliging him to reimburse the costs of the re‑treatment carried out by the second specialist? (3) Can Dr. Rivera nonetheless claim fees for the partially performed interventions or for the placement of a permanent inlay that ultimately was not completed, or must he refund amounts paid by the patient?

A mother (N), a Ghanaian national, left the family apartment in H (Germany) on 12 February 2018, taking her two minor children B (born 2014) and C (born 2016) without the father’s consent, although the father (M) had acknowledged both children shortly after their births. Before that move the family had lived for more than one year in H and, on 3 November 2017, the father had applied to the district court of L (Germany) asking for the children’s habitual residence to be fixed in H and for joint parental authority to be established; on 21 June 2018 that court declared German jurisdiction, fixed the children’s residence with the father and recorded the exercise of parental authority jointly. After leaving Germany the mother applied to courts in Z (Switzerland) on 5 March 2018 for provisional maintenance advances; the Swiss authority granted interim advances while the father contested jurisdiction, alleging that the relocation was unlawful and that the German courts had exclusive competence. In light of private international law and the relevant international conventions, in particular the Lugano Convention and the rules on child abduction, were the Swiss judicial authorities competent to order the provisional maintenance measures requested by the mother, or did jurisdiction belong exclusively to the German courts?

A Spanish-born man M., who married N. in Spain in 1975 and is the parent of A. and B., entered into a second marriage in 2010 in Canada with P. despite a civil-register extract from Spain showing no divorce from N. He died in 2012 in Canada; a Canadian probate authority, by an order dated 11 March 2015, recognized the validity of that second marriage and, by letters of administration dated 22 June 2015, appointed P. as personal representative of his estate. P. asked a Zurich bank for disclosure of the deceased’s account statements and related documents; the bank refused, invoking the absence of an apostille on the foreign instruments and the risk that honouring the foreign instruments would give effect in Switzerland to a potential situation of bigamy. N. and the children oppose recognition in Switzerland. Must the cantonal court recognize and declare enforceable in Switzerland the foreign order and the letters of administration so as to permit the requested accounting by P., notwithstanding the asserted conflict with Swiss public policy (notably possible bigamy) and despite the lack of an apostille, and which private international law and banking-law rules apply to resolve this question?

An undocumented migrant who remained in Switzerland without a residence permit for a period in the mid-2010s is accused, together with two associates, of committing a string of break-ins and thefts over roughly fourteen months. On 21 August 2017 the three booked a room above a roadside guesthouse after scouting the location; during the night two companions threatened and incapacitated the night clerk with weapons, bound him and forced open several customer safe-deposit boxes. The accused says he only acted as the driver, was unaware of any weapons and was coerced by the others. Biological traces matching him were found on a glove and on a pry bar used in the incident, and surveillance footage plus the clerk’s identification portray him as the organiser. Under Swiss criminal law, can he be lawfully convicted as a co‑author of qualified robbery (brigandage qualifié) rather than merely as a participant in gang theft or simple theft, and may a later fact‑finder uphold that qualification and a seven‑year sentence by re‑evaluating the facts and evidence within the meaning of Art. 398 StPO and, under Art. 398 StPO, in light of the principle in dubio pro reo?

A Geneva trust and corporate-services firm (Aurora Trustees SA) asserts that it assisted in negotiating and implementing a software distribution agreement concluded between NOVA Tech Holdings (a Panamanian company formed to commercialize a proprietary software) and an entity identified in the contract as “HORIZON GLOBAL LIMITED of Grand Cayman”, the agreement having been signed in March 2022 by an individual (Mr. R) who held himself out as “Managing Director”. NOVA paid three supplier invoices amounting to CHF 88,720 and Aurora incurred internal costs and disbursements of CHF 119,450.35; NOVA subsequently assigned its claims in writing to Aurora in July 2023. Aurora brings proceedings in Switzerland against Mr. R (resident in the canton of Fribourg) and Meridian SA (a company with its registered office in Geneva) seeking repayment of Aurora’s advances and costs and the removal of oppositions/encumbrances, while Mr. R and Meridian invoke a forum-selection clause in Article 12.04 of the agreement specifying the law of the State of Delaware and the courts of Wilmington and/or the exclusive jurisdiction of that foreign forum. Are the Geneva courts competent ratione loci to hear (1) Aurora Trustees SA’s personal claim for costs and advances (CHF 119,450.35), and (2) the claim assigned by NOVA Tech Holdings for reimbursement of payments made (CHF 88,720), in light of the Swiss rules on jurisdiction (LFors, LDIP), the doctrine of an agent acting without authority (Art. 38 and 39 CO) and the validity/enforceability of a jurisdiction clause agreed between the assignor and a foreign contracting party?

A member cooperative (AlderCo-op) that provides its members with administrative billing and debt-collection support received the invoices of one of its member practitioners, an optometrist (Dr. M); over the period from March 2010 to August 2014 the cooperative advanced fifty payments totalling about USD 870,000 and kept a running current account in the practitioner’s name. The cooperative asserts that, after offsetting the proceeds it collected on the practitioner’s behalf, approximately USD 51,200 remains outstanding; the practitioner disputes that figure, contending the cooperative has not demonstrated the collections, that some sums were paid directly to him, and that older claims are time‑barred. The practitioner nonetheless wrote on 5 September 2014 that he was prepared to repay USD 13,450 in monthly instalments of USD 1,200, and subsequently executed a written repayment memorandum on 1 November 2014. Should the legal relationship between Alder‑Coop and the optometrist be characterised as factoring or instead as a mixed contract (collection mandate + loan + current account), and what consequence would that characterisation have for prescription; did the cooperative meet its burden to prove the asserted USD 51,200 (in particular with regard to the duty to render accounts (Art. 400 OR)); and is the practitioner bound by the acknowledgement/novation of the USD 13,450 balance despite his ongoing contestations?

Is a midlands temporary‑staffing company, which is a member of a regional employers' association, obliged under a sectoral collective labour agreement, cantonal and federal law and federal jurisprudence to top up the pay of temporary production operatives assigned to a meal‑packing client to the higher local “customary wage” or to any minimum wage above the gross hourly rates set in their assignment contracts (for example CHF 17.50 or CHF 24.00), where: (a) the assignment contracts specified gross hourly wages that expressly included holiday and public‑holiday pay and part of an extra annual payment and were later amended upward in certain instances; (b) the collective agreement contains a provision excluding application of its minimum‑wage article for particular branches (including food processing) and refers instead to the customary wages fixed by cantonal tripartite bodies; (c) that collective agreement was extended and declared binding at federal level for a limited period; and (d) the temporary workers are not members of any organisation party to the collective agreement and have not provided a written submission to be bound by it?

L., a floor manager at Q. AG insured for accidents with R. AG, complained of right shoulder pain on 3 March 2023 during a recreational badminton game. The employer's accident report of 10 March 2023 and L.'s answers to a medical questionnaire dated 15 March 2023 state that the activity occurred in the usual course and that no particular event (in particular no fall) took place; however, only after R. AG refused coverage did L. for the first time assert that he had slipped and landed on his shoulder after a jump, subsequently producing a physician's certificate and a report by Dr S. describing that fall. An MRI performed on 12 March 2023 revealed active acromioclavicular osteoarthritis and a partial tear of a rotator cuff tendon, and R. AG's medical consultant considered the pathology to be a degenerative tendinopathy. In light of these initially consistent but later contradictory statements, the imaging findings and the medical opinions, does the shoulder condition of 3 March 2023 constitute an "accident" within the meaning of Art. 4 ATSG giving entitlement to benefits (Art. 6 Abs. 1 UVG), or, subsidiarily, a lesion assimilated to an accident within the meaning of Art. 6 Abs. 2 UVG, such that R. AG would be obliged to cover the treatment?

A Swiss husband (born 1940) and a wife (born 1948, holding two nationalities at the time) married in a Swiss mountain municipality in May 2002 under a separation‑of‑property regime. The wife alleges that, between mid‑2002 and April 2008, she withdrew cash from her foreign bank accounts and handed over bills totalling €100,500 (claimed equivalent CHF 160,000) to her husband so he would buy art and financial instruments in her name; the husband admits receiving the cash but says it was spent on household needs or lost when markets fell and did not keep detailed records or render an itemised accounting of how those monies were used. The spouses separated in 2009 and, in the ensuing divorce proceedings, the wife seeks restitution of CHF 160,000 (with interest from 15.10.2011) and, subsidiarily, a patrimonial settlement, and also claims post‑divorce maintenance on the basis that she stopped working when they married at an age over 60. Must the husband restitute CHF 160,000 (with interest), should the relationship between them be characterised as a fiduciary mandate or as a simple partnership for the purposes of liability and accounting, and is the wife entitled to a maintenance pension after the marriage ended?

Alex, married to Morgan since 1982, operates a small independent bakery in a coastal town and the partners separated on 20 August 2014. Alex produced a profit-and-loss statement as at 31 December 2014 showing a net operating profit of USD 45,120 and personal drawings of USD 72,500 recorded in a 'drawings account', while an earlier interim statement dated 30 September 2014 showed a net profit of USD 68,300; Morgan worked in the bakery (declared salary USD 2,500, raised to USD 4,200 in 2014) and received unemployment benefits from 1 September 2014 to 30 April 2015. A provisional maintenance order under protective measures required Alex to pay USD 2,150 per month; Alex disputes that calculation, claiming a lower net profit for 2014, asserting that certain essential non-disposable charges (mortgage installments, taxes and mandatory insurance) reduce his disposable income, and asking that expenses tied to a secondary holiday property not be imputed to him. In these circumstances, should the provisional maintenance contribution for a self-employed payer be determined by averaging net operating profits over the last three to four years or by using the last year’s profit adjusted for personal withdrawals; which expense items qualify as non-disposable (incompressible) deductible charges; and to what extent may a hypothetical income be imputed to Morgan for the period after 30 April 2015?

A commercial landlord, OceanGate Properties Ltd., and two occupants, Mr. Mason and Ms. Ortega, signed on 15 March 2020 a tenancy for a street-level retail kiosk (gross monthly rent CHF 2,200) together with a separate contract for a storage locker (CHF 95). The occupants accumulated rent arrears from the outset; the landlord sent registered formal notices on 5 September 2021 demanding CHF 5,100 for the kiosk (and CHF 285 for the locker), allowing a 30‑day cure period and warning that the tenancy would be terminated if unpaid. The registered letters were not collected and were returned after the postal retention period; the landlord served a formula termination on 10 October 2021, effective 31 October 2021, and sought summary eviction under the “cas clairs” procedure. On appeal the occupants produced, for the first time, bank statements from the tenancy period and asserted they only owed CHF 3,200. Are the formula termination and the summary eviction order valid, and, subsidiarily, may the bank statements filed first on appeal be admitted as nova and, if so, could they lead to annulment of the eviction order?

A Swiss electronics supplier, Helios Components AG, which is not a party to ongoing foreign litigation in a U.S. district in the Pacific Northwest, received an international judicial assistance request transmitted by a cantonal public prosecutor asking for the production of confidential materials (including firmware source code, technical specification sheets and license agreements) concerning microcontroller modules alleged to infringe patents. A cantonal judge issued an order on 12 September 2014 directing Helios to produce those materials within a specified period and to give a confidentiality undertaking, but the order did not advise the company in advance of any right to refuse cooperation nor did it state the remedies or the deadlines for seeking review. The company filed a late legal challenge asserting that the order is an order for the taking of evidence subject to immediate review, that the failure to indicate available remedies and time‑limits warrants admitting a late challenge on good‑faith grounds, and that its right to be heard was violated because it was not invited to assert a refusal right grounded in business/trade secrecy. Are (1) an order compelling a non‑party to produce documents in response to an international judicial assistance request to be treated as an order for taking of evidence susceptible to immediate review; (2) the omission to specify available remedies and review deadlines capable of rendering a belated challenge admissible on the basis of protection of good faith; and (3) the issuing authority required to invite the non‑party to state its position on a potential right to refuse (for example business secrecy) before ordering execution of the assistance request?

A married couple of Moroccan nationality, Ms. Farah and Mr. Samir, wed in 2008 and lived together in a small Swiss lakeside municipality; they have one child born in 2011. Mr. Samir started divorce proceedings in Morocco on 12 February 2012 and, after lower‑level and appellate stages abroad, the divorce was formally registered with a Moroccan notarial registry on 18 May 2015. While those foreign proceedings were pending, Ms. Farah applied to a competent Swiss judicial authority on 22 June 2013 seeking urgent protective relief in the form of provisional spousal maintenance, alleging she lacked sufficient income; the Swiss authority granted provisional measures and later faced the question whether to give effect in Switzerland to the Moroccan divorce instrument. In those circumstances, (1) may a Swiss judicial authority grant and continue provisional spousal relief during the period in which divorce proceedings between the same spouses are pending in their common national state, and (2) may Swiss authorities recognise and make enforceable a foreign divorce decree that was rendered without the parties appearing personally (the respondent having been represented by counsel), without running afoul of Swiss public policy?

A landowner commissioned construction of a seaside guesthouse and appointed an independent project manager; the owner signed a fixed-price building agreement with a contractor (HarborBuild Ltd.) for USD 245,000 including taxes to cover the structural work, a basement garage and an outdoor pool, with a contractual completion date of 30 June 2018 and a liquidated damages clause of 1% per working day for delays. The contractor performed the works, requested reinforcement plans and material lists several weeks before installation, received some documents belatedly, and submitted invoices for extra work after the owner requested modifications during the build; the project manager challenged certain charges and applied deductions, while separate subcontractors later had to redo or complete some finishing trades. The contractor obtained provisional registration of a statutory building mortgage for the unpaid balance and later applied for its definitive registration; the owner contests the sum claimed, cites delay, alleged defects and excessive charges for the extras, and requests an expert appraisal of the account. In those circumstances, can the contractor obtain definitive registration of the statutory mortgage as security for its claim despite the owner’s disputes about performance, deadlines and the rates charged for additional work, and should an expert appraisal have been ordered to resolve the project manager’s disputed accounts?

A Swiss importer (Alpine Trade GmbH) specialising in luxury watches hired a Geneva freight arranger (Nordic Logistics SA) to organise, in Alpine Trade's name and on its behalf, carriage of a consignment of 42 watch cases from Costa Rica to Zurich. Nordic Logistics, acting on a local recommendation, appointed a Costa Rican sub‑forwarder (Marina Cargo S.A., managed by Mr. R.) to receive, relabel and arrange onward transport; for four earlier consignments the explicit instruction not to transit through the United States was complied with. On a dispatch dated 11 August 2017 the shipment was loaded onto a flight that stopped in Miami, where US authorities seized the cargo and it never reached Zurich. Alpine Trade claims the value of the goods from Nordic Logistics, arguing that Nordic must answer for the acts of the sub‑forwarder as if it were an auxiliary; Nordic Logistics contends that the arrangement was a sub‑mandate and that its liability is limited to the care taken in selecting and instructing the sub‑mandatary, not to supervising its performance. In light of these facts, is the freight arranger (Nordic Logistics SA) contractually obliged to compensate Alpine Trade for the loss as a forwarding commission agent/carrier, or can it be exonerated because it resorted to a sub‑forwarder under the rules on mandate (notably Arts. 398 Abs. 3 OR and 399 Abs. 2 OR)?

In a cross-border family dispute, a foreign couple married in 1995 in Country Z have three children born in the suburb of Riverton in 1998, 2001 and 2003; the spouses signed a separation agreement on 12 June 2010 stipulating that the children would live with the father and that the courts of Lausanne would have jurisdiction. On 20 September 2011 the husband retained an attorney and initiated divorce proceedings on the basis of that agreement before a court in the Kingdom of Al-Basar, but service on the wife in that foreign forum was defective. The children were relocated abroad in November 2011 and later registered at a private school in Al-Basar, while the mother filed on 3 November 2011 in Lausanne for protective measures for the marriage and for protective measures concerning the children and also lodged a criminal complaint for unlawful removal of minors. In those circumstances, can Swiss judicial authorities order protective measures for the marital union and, more specifically, child-protection measures (custody, contact rights, conservatory measures) despite a divorce action pending abroad? In particular, should jurisdiction be derived from Art. 46 IPRG or, if recognition of the foreign divorce judgment is not a priori excluded, from Art. 10 IPRG (provisional measures), and does the fact that the children had their habitual residence in Lausanne at the time of filing allow the Lausanne courts to retain jurisdiction (principle of perpetuatio fori / 1996 Hague Convention)?

L. and M. served as co-signing members of the board of Helios Logistics SA, entered in the commercial register on 12 March 2006; L. resigned from the board in mid-2007 (deregistration published 15 September 2007) and the company was wound up by the authorities in February 2008 and removed from the register in July 2010. During 2006–2007 two warehouse employees were paid salaries; the Geneva cantonal compensation office for AHV issued contribution notices dated 10 April 2010 and 22 November 2010 (addressed to the company but notified directly to L.), and then, by decision dated 5 March 2011, sought CHF 25,300 from the two former board members by way of compensation under Art. 52 Abs. 1 AHVG. L. contends that the 2010 notices are null because they were not validly served on the company of which he was no longer an organ, that he did not carry out the administrative tasks (payroll and contribution remittance) so that the doctrine of differentiated solidarity should limit any liability, and that his right to be heard was violated (separate proceedings, refusal to call witnesses and to appoint an accounting expert); the question is whether, and to what extent, L. can be held liable, in his capacity as a former director, for the loss claimed by the cantonal AHV office arising from the non-payment of the 2006–2007 contributions?

Alex and Morgan Bell, married in 1994 and living apart since August 2017, obtained an emergency maintenance order in April 2019 that assessed Morgan's self-employed income (artisan chocolatier) at about $2,200 per month (net profit roughly $900). Alex commenced divorce proceedings in November 2020 and contends that Morgan actually earns substantially more (submitting invoices and a trade card), while Morgan disputes this and claims a 50% reduction in earning capacity for medical reasons. The first-instance judge, without ordering additional fact-finding, accepted the earlier income estimate; both spouses sought further review, with Alex specifically requesting witness testimony and asking that the division of marital assets be deferred. The questions are therefore: in divorce proceedings where the parties dispute a spouse’s earning capacity and an asserted medical incapacity, must the adjudicator apply the ordinary civil standard of proof (requiring that contested facts be established with convincing certainty) and, where there are objective matters that can be clarified, initiate or order evidentiary measures (for example, witness hearings or medical inquiries) rather than rely on the lower plausibility assessment used in emergency maintenance proceedings? Separately, may a reviewing judge consider a request to defer the liquidation/division of marital property when that request was not raised before the initial fact-finder?

A couple married in 1976 without a marital property contract separated and later initiated divorce proceedings following a unilateral petition filed on 15 June 2010. They are co-owners of a seaside villa in Spain purchased in 1992 with funds from the spouses’ joint income; the former wife asks the Swiss divorce court to order the immediate sale of that villa and an equal division of the net proceeds, and also claims one half of a CHF 95,000 insurance payout the husband received after a traffic accident and one half of the value of a car the husband bought in 2009. The trial authority declined to entertain the request to compel sale of the foreign immovable on private international law grounds and declined to decide on attribution of the property; it treated the CHF 95,000 as a presumed marital asset for lack of documentary proof and dismissed the claim as to the car’s value for want of probative evidence. The key questions are these: may a Swiss divorce court, in the course of liquidating the matrimonial property regime, order the sale or otherwise attribute an immovable located in Spain that the spouses co-own, or must the co-ownership be partitioned first before the marital estate is liquidated; and, separately, what evidentiary rules and presumptions govern treating an insurance indemnity and a movable acquired during the marriage as marital property (acquêts) and imposing their division between the spouses?

A married couple without a matrimonial agreement, Jordan and Priya Malik, separated in 2014; Priya was awarded sole custody of their four children (born 2006, 2009, 2011 and 2013) and Jordan was granted exclusive use of a co‑owned riverfront bungalow that the spouses had bought as joint owners. After the separation Jordan vacated the bungalow and, with Priya’s consent, let the property from February 2015 until August 2018 for USD 2,200 per month; Jordan applied the rent to secure alternative housing for himself and to meet the bungalow’s running costs. Priya now claims (a) one half of the rental income paid to Jordan for that letting period, (b) a post‑divorce maintenance allowance in her favour, (c) an adjustment or increase of the regular child maintenance payments, and (d) that Jordan be ordered to contribute to extraordinary child expenses (notably psychotherapy and certain dental/orthodontic treatments). In light of the rules governing matrimonial property, the law of obligations and family/parentage law: (1) can Priya recover one half of the rents received by Jordan for the period indicated; (2) under what conditions would a post‑divorce maintenance contribution be due to Priya; and (3) how should the routine child maintenance contributions be fixed and under what conditions can special contributions (psychotherapy, dental/orthodontic costs) be imposed on the parent ordered to pay maintenance?

In December 2024, two separated parents alternated short-term custody of their two children, L. (11 years) and S. (7 years), with the children's official residence originally registered with the mother. A municipal Family Assessment Unit prepared a professional welfare report finding that the alternating arrangement had become detrimental to the children, noting sanitary shortcomings at the mother's home and concluding that the father was presently better able to meet the children's daily care needs; a pediatric examination recorded genital injuries and an anal fissure in the younger child, and a Child Protection Response Team alerted the police to suspected sexual abuse allegedly occurring while the child was in the mother's care. On that basis a provisional order temporarily transferred sole custody to the father, established a guardianship for educational support and supervised contact, and prohibited the mother from placing the younger child in the company of a particular third person who was the subject of related criminal allegations. The mother sought an expedited review of those provisional measures, requesting among other things a fresh direct interview of the children and restoration of custody to her. In an expedited review context and in light of the professional family-assessment report and the risk that a new direct interview would create a loyalty conflict for very young children, may the reviewing authority decline to order an additional hearing of children who were already interviewed by professionals and uphold the provisional transfer of custody to the father as compatible with the children's best interests and the governing legal criteria?

A 23-year-old visitor goes late at night to her sister's art studio in a three-storey mixed-use building owned by a property management company. The building's street entrance, installed in the 1960s, has two swing leaves glazed with 5 mm float glass panes set in an aluminium frame; after 8 p.m. the door opens only by key or keypad and on the night of the incident the electronic entry system was not working. About fourteen days earlier a technician had readjusted the door closer because the door had been slamming; the visitor claims that she hooked the bottom of the leaf with her foot, whereupon a pane fell or shattered and inflicted a serious laceration to her thigh. The panel was subsequently exchanged for laminated safety glazing and an inspection report prepared four years later observed corrosion and several missing screws in the frame. Can the property owner be held liable under Art. 58 Abs. 1 OR for a construction defect or deficient maintenance that caused these injuries — in particular because ordinary float glass was used on a heavily used entry door — and do the age of the door or the visitor’s forceful contact with it exclude or diminish that liability?

An adult born in 1969, living in a small coastal community and severely disabled after a prior stroke (very limited mobility, largely non‑verbal and communicating mainly by typed messages on a tablet) who relies on several in‑home care providers saw their insurer cut back benefits, producing mounting unpaid bills. Local care coordinators and the treating physician reported persistent administrative problems and raised concerns about a possible underlying psychiatric condition, asking that a formal welfare inquiry be opened and that a guardian be appointed; a local magistrate ordered the opening of an inquiry, directed a psychiatric expert assessment and provisionally appointed a guardian with powers for representation and financial management. The person appealed, arguing among other things that their impairments are purely physical, that earlier psychiatric reports were favourable to them, and that a limited accompaniment‑style guardianship would be adequate. The issues are: (1) is an appeal against the opening of the inquiry and against the order for a psychiatric expert assessment admissible — specifically, does the mandatory expert assessment create an injury that is difficult to repair such that an immediate appeal is justified — and (2) in light of the medical facts and the administrative/financial circumstances (multiple bank accounts, outstanding invoices of roughly $16,200, practical inability to complete oral or in‑person formalities, and refusal of psychiatric treatment), was the provisional guardianship for representation and management lawful, necessary and proportionate under adult protection law?

A., born in 1987, an operations manager and co-tenant of a riverside townhouse, and B., born in 1986, currently unemployed and mother of a child from a prior relationship, married in 2020. In 2023 the tenancy for the shared residence was terminated with a vacate date of 30 June 2023; the parties initially agreed to leave by that date but B. continued to occupy the property past 30 June 2023. A. had been removed from the premises by police in February 2023 and, in expedited interim proceedings dealing with protective measures in the matrimonial sphere, requested to be relieved of any obligation to pay maintenance to B. and to have B.'s right to remain in the home ended. The first-instance judge ordered periodic maintenance for B. and, from a specified earlier date, imputed a notional employment income to her retroactively; B. contests the retroactive imputation, arguing she could not reasonably have resumed paid work so quickly (pointing to a scheduled vocational course and an asserted pregnancy) and also disputes the way housing costs and rent were apportioned between the parties. In summary/interim matrimonial-protection proceedings, is it lawful for the court to impute a hypothetical income to B. effective as of the original vacate date (or must an adaptation period be granted before such notional earnings are attributed), and how should the maintenance contribution and the division between the spouses of the statutory subsistence minimum and any surplus be calculated in light of the circumstances (age, education and skills, health, existence of non-common children, the actual housing situation and rental burdens, and the evidentiary material submitted)?

C.L., born in 1950, and D.L., born in 1955, married in 1977 and have lived apart since March 2001; they have two grown children. After the separation, D.L. returned to part-time employment but subsequently had spells of unemployment and limited capacity for work due to chronic spinal and hip problems; D.L.'s net annual earnings were about CHF 46,800 in 2008 (roughly CHF 3,900/month), CHF 49,200 in 2009 (roughly CHF 4,100/month) and thereafter consisted of short-term contracts, unemployment benefits and periods of incapacity, producing an estimated average net income of approximately CHF 3,250/month by 2011. C.L. works full time, receives a net pay of CHF 6,200/month (including a 13th salary) and has a significant vested benefits claim; C.L.'s incompressible monthly expenses are estimated at CHF 3,600. In light of a long marriage (more than twenty years), a lengthy separation, D.L.'s health and the historical and current income pattern, and C.L.'s resources and unavoidable charges, does D.L. have a right to spousal maintenance under Art. 125 of the CC, and if so which method of computation should be used (the minimum-subsistence approach with distribution of any surplus, or preservation of the standard of living during the separation), and what fair monthly amount should be set taking into account the obligor's estimated earning capacity and essential outgoings?

A person, Ms. A. Reed (born 1982), who holds a long‑term resident permit and is unemployed while receiving reintegration benefits, is alleged to have, between May 2020 and April 2022, assumed other people’s identities to place numerous mail and online orders for goods and services totaling about USD 220,000–320,000 (including roughly twenty‑five travel bookings invoiced at about USD 85,000). She was taken into custody on 10 March 2024; the public prosecutor sought three months of pretrial detention citing a risk of flight (the suspect’s partner lives abroad in a state that does not extradite its nationals) and a risk of reoffending. Searches turned up unopened letters, payment cards and parcels in third parties’ names, and mobile data and messages still await forensic analysis. The defendant disputes the risk of flight, asserts deep local ties (resident for over 25 years, a mother living locally and the primary carer for an ill adult child) and proposes alternative measures: surrender of identity documents, deposit of her passport with authorities, daily reporting to the police station and outpatient treatment at a public addiction clinic. On these facts, could ordering provisional detention on the ground of flight risk be justified, and should detention have been maintained when assessed for necessity and proportionality in light of the suggested substitute measures?

A male trainee born in 1996 with a history of asthma completed a two‑year vocational apprenticeship in warehouse operations at Miro Logistics from 1 September 2015 to 31 August 2017. His pre‑employment medical record noted a sensitivity to dust. Throughout the program and associated placements he worked in large storage bays with airborne particulates and reports repeated contact with cleaning solvents; he further alleges that on 12 March 2016 a nearby tanker crash released corrosive chemicals and that he was exposed at that time. Beginning in spring 2016 he experienced recurrent nosebleeds and was later retrospectively diagnosed with granulomatosis with polyangiitis and placed on immunosuppressive therapy; he now claims to be disabled and seeks recognition and coverage as an occupational disease under the UVG, including compensation for any aggravation of his pre‑existing asthma. The statutory accident insurer (Omega Insurance) initially denied coverage. An administrative review of the file requested additional factual investigation (in particular to identify the nature of the dusts/solvents and to verify the extent and reality of any exposure from the tanker crash); Omega then made inquiries with the employer, examined the employment file and the police incident report and reaffirmed its denial. Question: Do the ENT disorders (granulomatosis with polyangiitis) and any worsening of the claimant’s asthma qualify as an occupational disease or as an occupational aggravation of a pre‑existing condition within the meaning of the UVG so that the insurer must provide benefits, and did Omega Insurance satisfy its investigatory obligations or should it have commissioned a multidisciplinary expert examination?

Under Art. 839 ZGB, is the registration of a craftsmen's and contractors' statutory lien valid where a limited company carrying out roofing and cladding work filed for registration on 21 September 2022 for CHF 62,310.50 against the owner of an industrial yard, given that the work was performed under an initial agreement of 10 April 2017, followed by a signed estimate on 2 October 2018 (roofing works for CHF 102,500.00 incl. VAT) and several supplementary orders invoiced in 2019, the invoices and measured quantities describing the services as 'executed' and the contractor's last on‑site intervention occurring at the latest in mid‑2020 — or must the four‑month period prescribed by Art. 839 ZGB be calculated from the definitive withdrawal/cessation of the contractor's performance, such that the 21 September 2022 application was either timely or late?

Two half-siblings, E.F. and G.H., are potential heirs after their parent died on 12 February 2017, leaving both real and personal property including items located in Switzerland. On 5 April 2017 a document titled 'Renunciation of inheritance', purportedly signed by power of attorney on behalf of E.F., was recorded in a small foreign jurisdiction. E.F. commenced legal proceedings in that foreign forum challenging the scope of the renunciation and asserting entitlement to the assets situated in Switzerland; those proceedings remained pending when Swiss authorities issued provisional conservatory measures to preserve movable assets in a Swiss locality. The Swiss order made the validity of those provisional measures contingent on the initiation of an action on the merits within a set deadline (15 October 2023) and later concluded the measures had lapsed because no separate action had been filed in Switzerland within that period. Under Art. 263 ZPO and Art. 89 IPRG, can the merits proceedings already pending abroad, which were begun before the Swiss order and concern the same dispute over the renunciation and rights to assets located in Switzerland, be treated as the 'action on the merits' required to validate the Swiss provisional measures and thereby prevent their lapse when no new suit was filed in Switzerland within the prescribed time limit?

A juvenile born in 2007 is alleged to have, on 9 November 2023 at about 6:20 a.m., entered the loading bay behind a small electronics shop where a young woman was present, kissed and sexually restrained her and taken her necklace; the complainant submitted a report on 15 January 2024. A second, similar assault in the same municipality with a comparable modus operandi was recorded by police on 2 March 2024. Officers obtained a buccal swab from the juvenile and, by an order dated 3 August 2024, the juvenile investigating judge ordered that a DNA profile be established from that sample and charged the costs to the file. The defence challenges the order, asserting it was not informed of the later incident, that no biological traces were recovered at the scene nor from the complainant in relation to the first report, that the judge’s assessment of similarities was inadequate to justify DNA profiling, and that the order infringes the accused’s right to be heard and the principle of proportionality. Was the juvenile judge competent to order the creation of the DNA profile, and does the order conform to the law (notably Art. 255 Abs. 1 StPO and Art. 197 Abs. 1 StPO), to the proportionality principle, and to the right to be heard/Art. 101 Abs. 1 StPO regarding access to the file?

A Swiss retailer, MOUNTAIN BIKES Sàrl (entered in the cantonal commercial register in 2003), has used the domain www.bikes-online.ch since October 2003 and marketed bicycles to Swiss customers at least from March 2004 with advertising campaigns. It is the proprietor of an international trademark that combines the verbal element BIKES-ONLINE.COM with a logo (registered at WIPO in 2006). A foreign company, RIVERVEND AG, registered on 12 September 2003 the domain bikeonline.net and thereafter, in 2004, several .ch domain names (www.bikesonline.ch, www.bikes-online.ch, www.bikeonline.ch, www.bike-online.ch) which it redirects to its Swiss-facing webshop cycledirect.ch where it offers bicycles for sale in Swiss francs; RIVERVEND asserts an earlier start of activities but produces no evidence of Swiss sales or advertising before August 2005. MOUNTAIN BIKES seeks in Switzerland an order prohibiting the use of those domain names and the transfer of the registrations, invoking the Trademark Protection Act (LPM / MSchG) and the Unfair Competition Act (LCD / UWG). RIVERVEND disputes the admissibility of a transfer remedy and argues that parts of the contested signs lack distinctiveness. Does RIVERVEND’s registration and use of these closely similar domain names amount to a substantiated infringement of trademark rights or an act of unfair competition within the meaning of the UWG entitling MOUNTAIN BIKES to an injunction against their use and, where appropriate, the transfer of the domain names in favour of MOUNTAIN BIKES in Switzerland?

A 29-year-old delivery driver had an intimate relationship with a former partner eight years his senior; during that relationship the partner allegedly caused physical injury on at least three separate occasions (medical entries dated 15 February 2013, 20 September 2013 and 3 May 2014), used insulting language and repeatedly harassed the claimant’s family (more than 320 recorded calls and text messages), conduct which led to criminal proceedings and a conviction for insults, threats and abusive use of a telecommunications service. The employee stopped reporting for work on 10 March 2014 and was dismissed on 9 April 2014 for absenteeism; the employee later produced a medical certificate dated 12 January 2015 certifying total incapacity for work from 10 March 2014 until 28 November 2014, notwithstanding that the employee spent several months abroad between June and September 2014. In civil proceedings the claimant seeks compensation for lost earnings (approx. CHF 62,400), reimbursement of medical expenses and repair of a damaged bicycle, and an award for non‑material (moral) damage (CHF 18,000). Having regard to the medical records, the alleged wrongful conduct, the stay abroad and the other evidence produced: (i) is the former partner civilly liable for the claimed loss of earnings and for moral damage; (ii) is there sufficient proof of adequate causation between his unlawful conduct and the claimed loss of earnings; and (iii) what amount would be an appropriate award for moral damage?

Two independent maritime consultants, C and D, worked together out of a shared waterfront studio in a mid-sized coastal town from March 2010 until their separation in August 2013 without any written agreement; each invoiced clients in their own name, kept separate accounting ledgers and, beginning in June 2011, orally agreed to split equally the revenues and the expenses arising from certain joint contracts, with monthly invoices issued by D and regular transfers made by C. After they ended the collaboration, D claims payment of an outstanding balance for shared fees and costs while C seeks reimbursement and alleges that the equal-sharing arrangements should be set aside for material mistake or fraud based on an accounting review prepared in April 2016. What (1) is the correct legal characterization of the relationship between C and D (simple partnership, mandate/agency, subcontracting or a sui generis collaborative contract), (2) how should the fees and expenses for the years 2011–2013 be established and allocated when the parties maintained separate books but exchanged invoices and payments, and (3) which party carries the burden of proof for the amounts claimed and for the alleged mistake/fraud relied on to challenge the agreements?

A Swiss woman (Sofia) married, on 12 September 2011 in Lucerne, a foreign national (Marco) who had been employed as a live‑in caregiver by a relative of hers and was accommodated and paid by that household; the parties signed a prenuptial agreement providing for separation of property and a waiver of inheritance rights, they never formed a common household nor pooled finances, and the husband later produced medical certificates alleging incapacity for work and brought claims against his employers before the labour authorities. In proceedings for protective measures for the marital union, the husband seeks a maintenance payment and a provisio ad litem on the basis of alleged lack of means, while the wife alleges the marriage was a sham concluded solely to secure a residence permit, asks for a declaration of abuse of rights and the dismissal of any maintenance obligation. Should the court conclude that the husband’s maintenance claim is barred as an abuse of rights because the marriage was contracted and preserved only to circumvent residence rules, there was no community of life nor reciprocal financial contributions, and, subsidiarily, would maintenance nevertheless be payable when assessed under the criteria of Art. 176 ZGB and the rules on protective measures for the marital union?
